elements.sent-2411-001: We consider systems of interacting particles which are described by a second order Langevin equation, i.e., particles experiencing inertia.
elements.sent-2411-002: We introduce an associated equation of fluctuating hydrodynamics, which can be interpreted as stochastic version of a Vlasov-Fokker-Planck equation.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that this stochastic partial differential equation exhibits the same dichotomy as the corresponding first order (inertial-free) equation, the so-called Dean-Kawasaki equation: Solutions exist only for suitable atomic initial data, but not for smooth initial data.
elements.sent-2411-004: The class of systems covered includes several models of active matter.
elements.section-2411-001: Introduction and statement of main result
elements.sent-2411-005: Equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics are stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs) describing the collective evolution of particles.
elements.sent-2411-006: They are widely used in physics, among others to describe active matter (e.g.,~\cite{Fodor2021a ).}
elements.sent-2411-007: The mathematical analysis of such equations is in its infancy, despite significant recent progress.
elements.sent-2411-008: Here we consider fluctuating hydrodynamics for Vlasov-Fokker-Planck type equations.
elements.sent-2411-009: To describe the context, we first sketch the situation for purely diffusive (Fokker-Planck) type of equations, as it is much better studied.
elements.sent-2411-010: The most prominent purely diffusive example is the Dean-Kawasaki equation, which is a field-theoretic mesoscopic description of interacting particle systems obeying overdamped (first order) Langevin dynamics~\cite{Dean1996a,Kawasaki1973a .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The resulting stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) is a singular supercritical SPDE with non-Lipschitz multiplicative noise.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is known~\cite{Konarovskyi2019a,Konarovskyi2020a that the equation is well-posed only for initial data given by suitable atomic measures, such as empirical measures.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Smoothened versions of the Dean-Kawasaki equation equation are analyzed in~\cite{Cornalba2019a, Djurdjevac2024a, Fehrman2024a, Mariani2010a , where it can be shown that more regular, spatially diffuse solutions exist.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this article, we discuss an analogue of the Dean-Kawasaki equations for fluid particles in the inertial (i.e., second order, not overdamped) regime.
elements.sent-2411-015: As main difference to previous works~\cite{Cornalba2021a,Cornalba2019a,Cornalba2020a,Lutsko2012a on such systems, we chose a parametrization of the fluid states by densities for the resulting particle field which depend on position and velocity, $(x,v)\in \R^d\times \R^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This allows us to derive a description of the system by a stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) in a straightforward way, without heuristic steps or assumptions on being in local equilibrium.

elements.section-2411-004: Introduction and statement of the main result
elements.sent-2411-240: Equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics can be thought of as the the mean-field limit of many particle systems (such as the Vlasov-Fokker-Planck equation) ``plus noise'', where the noise is of a particular structure, reflecting fluctuation-dissipation relations.
elements.sent-2411-241: Specifically, fluctuating hydrodynamics describes the evolution of finitely many particles (as opposed to the mean-field limit describing infinitely many particles).
elements.sent-2411-242: We show below that this description of many-particle dynamics is exact and can be derived mathematically using a martingale formulation.
elements.sent-2411-243: The argument given below is an alternative to noise replacement methods used in the physics literature~\cite{Dean1996a .}
elements.sent-2411-244: In this article we consider fluctuating hydrodynamics for Vlasov-Fokker-Planck type equations, i.e., hypoelliptic equations, which describe the evolution of second order Langevin equations (that is, evolution involving inertia).
elements.sent-2411-245: We show that for purely atomic initial data, such as empirical measures, solution exist in a martingale sense, while provably no solutions exist for smooth initial data.
elements.sent-2411-246: This dichotomy between existence and nonexistence was previously only known for purely diffusive equations~\cite{Konarovskyi2019a,Konarovskyi2020a , i.e., Focker-Planck equation describing the evolution of first order Langevin equations, without inertia.}
elements.sent-2411-247: One interpretation is that the equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics considered here describe exactly the dynamics of an arbitrary number of particles experiencing second order Langevin dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-248: Specifically, the result of this papers covers the collective evolution of $n$ particles given by second order Langevin equations of the type \begin{equation}\dd x_t^i = v_t^i \dd t, \dd v_t^i = - \gamma v_t^i \dd t - \nabla U(x_t^i) \dd t - \sum_{j =1}^n f(x_t^i,x_t^j) -\frac 1 n \sum_{j=1}^n \nabla H(v_t^i - v_t^j) \dd t + \sqrt{2} \dd W_t^i,\end{equation} where $U$ and $H$ are potentials and $f$ describes particle interaction and $W_t^i$ are independent Brownian motions.
elements.formula-2411-069: \begin{equation}\dd x_t^i = v_t^i \dd t, \dd v_t^i = - \gamma v_t^i \dd t - \nabla U(x_t^i) \dd t - \sum_{j =1}^n f(x_t^i,x_t^j) -\frac 1 n \sum_{j=1}^n \nabla H(v_t^i - v_t^j) \dd t + \sqrt{2} \dd W_t^i,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-249: More general settings, including models of active matter, are considered in Section~\ref{sec.active-matter .}
elements.sent-2411-250: To describe the context of this work, we sketch the situation for purely diffusive (Fokker-Planck) type of equations, as it is much better studied.
elements.sent-2411-251: Here we show that hypoelliptic equations of the type~\eqref{eqn:DKE exhibit the same rigidity in terms of well-posedness as the standard Dean-Kawasaki equation~\cite{Konarovskyi2019a,Konarovskyi2020a .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: After the results of~\cite{Konarovskyi2019a,Konarovskyi2020a , it was initially not clear whether the dichotomy (existence for atomic initial data, nonexistence for smooth initial data) is a peculiarity of purely diffusive Dean-Kawasaki equations.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Recently, Dello Schiavo~\cite{Dello-Schiavo2024aTR has shown that this phenomenon holds for a very wide class of diffusive systems.}
elements.sent-2411-254: This complements the analysis of this paper, which focuses on the hypoelliptic situation, i.e., particles experiencing inertia.
elements.sent-2411-255: The combination of these results gives now a much clearer picture of the situation: The dichotomy between existence (for atomic initial data) and nonexistence (for other initial data) holds for a very wide class of equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics (in fact, we are not aware of an equation of fluctuating hydrodynamics where it does not hold).
elements.sent-2411-256: The dichotomy has immediate practical implications.
elements.sent-2411-257: It shows that the equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics, including~\eqref{eqn:DKE , covered by this result are very unstable: Every attempt to smooth the initial data with a (possibly small) mollifier immediately destroys the existence of a solution.}
elements.sent-2411-258: One possibility is thus to consider smoothened versions of the Dean-Kawasaki equation, as analyzed in~\cite{Cornalba2019a, Djurdjevac2024a, Fehrman2024a, Mariani2010a .}
elements.sent-2411-259: Then it can be shown that more regular, spatially diffuse solutions exist.
elements.sent-2411-260: For equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics without regularisation in the noise, however, particle methods need to be employed in the simulation, as in~\cite{Embacher2018a,Huang2025a ; methods assuming the existence of a smooth solution profile such as finite difference or finite volume methods are not applicable.}
elements.sent-2411-261: Before presenting our rigorous results we sketch the derivation an equation of type~\eqref{eqn:DKE .}
elements.sent-2411-262: The approach we present here is straightforward, but puts the sometimes indirect derivation of such equations in the physics literature on form mathematical ground.
elements.sent-2411-263: To work with a specific situation, we consider the case of particles obeying \begin{equation}\dd x_t^i = v_t^i\dd t, \label{deriv-a} \dd v_t^i = -\frac 1 n \sum_{j=1}^n \nabla H(v_t^i - v_t^j) \dd t + \dd W_t^i, \label{deriv-b}\end{equation} for $i = 1, \ldots, n$, where $x_t^i \in \R^d$ and $v_t^i \in \R^d$.

elements.sent-2411-282: We consider systems of interacting particles which are described by a second order Langevin equation.
elements.sent-2411-283: The class of equations considered includes the situation where the particle evolution is governed by Hamiltonian dynamics with additional damping and noise satisfying a fluctuation-dissipation relation.
elements.sent-2411-284: Also covered are systems of two equations describing an evolution of interacting agents, as arising in several descriptions of active matter, including models for flocking and swarming.
elements.sent-2411-285: We first show that such particle systems can be represented exactly by so-called equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics, which in this case are stochastic versions of a Vlasov-Fokker-Planck type equation.
elements.sent-2411-286: While the derivation given here is simple, it is a blueprint for the rigorous derivation of equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-287: We then show a dichotomy previously known for purely diffusive (first order) systems carries over to the second order setting considered here: Solutions exist for suitable atomic initial data, in which case the solution is, properly scaled, the empirical density describing the particle system.
elements.sent-2411-288: For smooth initial data, however, we prove that no solution exists.
elements.sent-2411-289: They are widely used in physics, among others to describe active matter (e.g.,~\cite{Fodor2022a ).}
elements.sent-2411-290: Equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics can be thought of as the the mean-field limit of many particle systems (such as the Vlasov-Fokker-Planck equation) ``plus noise'', where the noise is of a particular structure, reflecting a fluctuation-dissipation relation.
elements.sent-2411-291: Specifically, fluctuating hydrodynamics is a mesoscopic description of interacting particle systems (as opposed to the mean-field limit describing infinitely many particles).
elements.sent-2411-292: The argument given below is a precise mathematical derivation, complementing the noise replacement methods used in the physics literature~\cite{Dean1996a .}
elements.sent-2411-293: This method is applied here to derive new equations describing a large variety of models.
elements.sent-2411-294: We consider fluctuating hydrodynamics for Vlasov-Fokker-Planck type equations, including hypoelliptic equations, which describe the evolution of second order Langevin equations (that is, evolution involving inertia).
elements.sent-2411-295: This dichotomy between existence and nonexistence was previously only known for purely diffusive equations~\cite{Konarovskyi2019a,Konarovskyi2020a , i.e., Fokker-Planck equations describing the evolution of particles undergoing first order Langevin dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-296: For instance, the result of this papers covers the collective evolution of $n$ particles given by second order Langevin equations of the type \begin{equation}\dd x_t^i = v_t^i \dd t, \dd v_t^i = - \gamma v_t^i \dd t -\frac 1 n \sum_{j=1}^n \nabla G(x_t^i - x_t^j) \dd t + \sqrt{\gamma} \dd W_t^i,\end{equation} where $G$ is an interaction potential and $W_t^i$ are independent Brownian motions.

elements.sent-2411-297: More general settings, including interaction terms depending on the velocities and nonlinear dependence in the first equation, are considered in Section~\ref{sec.active-matter .}
elements.sent-2411-298: The class of models covered in this setting includes various models of active matter, see Section~\ref{sec:examples .}

elements.sent-2411-302: The aforementioned dichotomy, established in Theorem~\ref{thm:mainthm , shows that equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics are not well posed in the sense of Cauchy, in fact are very unstable in the sense that even small smoothing of atomic data with a kernel function immediately renders the equation insoluble.}
elements.sent-2411-303: One interpretation of this result is that equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics are an exact encoding of the underlying particle system, and not meaningful for other initial data.
elements.sent-2411-304: While particle systems and associated equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics thus encode the same setting and are in this sense equivalent, they can offer different advantages.
elements.sent-2411-305: For example, the numerical simulation of equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics can be much more efficient than the simulation of the underlying particle model~\cite{Helfmann2021a,Cornalba2023a,Cornalba2023bTR .}
elements.sent-2411-306: Also, equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics are related to large deviation principles in a way which reveals the geometric structure of the associated hydrodynamic limit~\cite[Section 3.5]{Jack2014a .}
elements.sent-2411-307: In this way, fluctuating hydrodynamics can be useful to identify a path integral representation of the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-308: Equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics are also central to learn the ``thermodynamic'' evolution operator (such as the Wasserstein operator for diffusion) from particle data~\cite{Li2019a .}
elements.sent-2411-309: We first review the situation for purely diffusive (Fokker-Planck) type of equations, as it is much better studied.
elements.sent-2411-310: The most prominent purely diffusive example is the Dean-Kawasaki equation, which is a field-theoretic mesoscopic description of interacting particle systems obeying overdamped (first order) Langevin dynamics~\cite{Dean1996a,Kawasaki1973a , cf.~\cite{illien2025 for a recent survey.}}
elements.sent-2411-311: Here we show that this phenomenon holds for a very wide class of equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics (in fact, we are not aware of an equation of fluctuating hydrodynamics in the sense considered here where it does not hold).
elements.sent-2411-312: As mentioned above, the dichotomy has immediate practical implications.
elements.sent-2411-313: It shows that the equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics, including~\eqref{intro.VLPDK , covered by this result are very unstable: Every attempt to smoothen the initial data with a (possibly small) mollifier immediately destroys the existence of a solution.}
elements.sent-2411-314: A rigorous justification of diffusive fluctuating hydrodynamics for $N$ particles by numerical discretizations to arbitrary order in $N^{-1}$ is given in~\cite{Cornalba2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-315: Furthermore, see~\cite{Angeli2024aTR for the approximation of McKean-Vlasov equations with additive noise by weighted particle systems.}
elements.sent-2411-316: We close this section with a brief, and certainly incomplete, discussion of equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics in different application areas.
elements.sent-2411-317: Variants of the Dean-Kawasaki equation~\cite{Dean1996a as the prototypical equation of fluctuating hydrodynamics are widely used in very different application areas.}
elements.sent-2411-318: In fluid mechanics, they appear as models of hydrodynamic fluctuation theory, for example for thin film models~\cite{Grun2006a and colloidal nucleation theory~\cite{Lutsko2012a .}}
elements.sent-2411-319: Dean-Kawasaki type models exist in dynamical density functional theory~\cite{Vrugt2020b .}
elements.sent-2411-320: There are also Dean-Kawasaki models for systems, such as the Keller-Segel model~\cite{Martini2024aTR .}

elements.sent-2411-322: Dean-Kawasaki equations also appear as models for crowd behaviour, as alternative to agent-based simulations~\cite{Helfmann2021a .}
elements.sent-2411-323: The stochastic gradient descent used in Machine Learning can be viewed as a Dean--Kawasaki type process, as shown and analysed in~\cite{Rotskoff2022a,Gess2022aTR .}

elements.section-2411-007: Derivation of equations of fluctuating hydrodynamics
elements.sent-2411-325: We now sketch the derivation of an equation of type~\eqref{intro.VLPDK from a particle system undergoing Langevin dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-326: The approach we present here is straightforward, but has in our view two benefits: Firstly, it puts the sometimes indirect derivation of related equations in the purely diffusive setting in the physics literature on firm mathematical ground.
elements.sent-2411-327: Secondly, it shows that the resulting system of fluctuating hydrodynamics is an exact representation of the underlying particle system, without any approximations involved.
elements.sent-2411-328: We describe the evolution with a probability measure \begin{equation}\label{deriv-delta} \mu_t(x,v) = \frac 1 n \sum_{i=1}^n \delta_{(x_t^i, v_t^i)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-329: This seems very natural, but marks the crucial departure from previous work~\cite{Cornalba2021a,Cornalba2019a,Cornalba2020a on such systems, where reduced models, where position and time are the only independent variables.}
elements.sent-2411-330: We chose a description of the system by a density which depends on the position and the velocity of the particles, $(x,v)\in \R^d\times \R^d$.
